§ 396. An impeachment is the prosecution, by the house of representatives, before the senate, of the governor or other civil officer, for misdemeanor in office.

§ 397. The articles of impeachment are the written accusation of the officer, drawn up and approved by the house of representatives.

§ 398. The articles of impeachment shall state with reasonable certainty the misdemeanor in office for which the officer is impeached; and if there be more than one they shall be stated separately and distinctly.

§ 399. When the articles of impeachment have been approved by the house of representatives, and an impeachment ordered, a committee shall be appointed to prosecute it, whose

# IMPEACHMENTS

Chairman, within five days, shall lay the same before the senate.

§ 400. The senate shall appoint a day for hearing the impeachment, and the accused shall be summoned, by a precept issued by the clerk of the senate, to appear on that day. The precept shall be served by delivering a copy of the precept, and of the articles of impeachment, to the accused in person, if to be found, or leaving the copies at his residence with some white member of his family over sixteen years of age.

§ 401. The clerk of the senate, at the request of the chairman or of the accused, shall issue process for summoning witnesses, and for producing books and papers; and in case of disobedience of the process, the senate shall order the clerk to issue process for arresting the witnesses and seizing the books and papers, which process may be executed by the peace officers of the several counties, or by officers specially appointed for that purpose by the senate.

§ 402. The senate shall have power of coercing the attendance of witnesses, and of compelling them to testify, and of coercing the production of books and papers, by fine and imprisonment, to such an extent as shall be necessary.

§ 403. Witnesses shall have the same compensation for travel and attendance, and the same exemptions in going, remaining, and returning, as witnesses in the circuit courts, and officers executing the process and orders of the senate shall have like fees for their services.

§ 404. Before the senate proceeds to try the impeachment, the speaker and every senator present shall take the following oath or affirmation: "I do solemnly swear (or affirm) that I will faithfully and impartially try the impeachment against A B, and give my decision according to the law and evidence."

§ 405. The senate shall determine what amount of absence

FOR THE REMOVAL FROM OFFICE OF CLERKS OF COURTS.

of a senator during the trial shall exclude the senator from voting in the final decision.

§ 406. If the accused is acquitted, he shall be entitled to his costs, to be taxed by the clerk of the senate and paid by the treasurer; and if convicted he shall pay the costs, to be taxed by the clerk and recovered by motion by the attorney general, in the Franklin circuit court, at the first term thereof, without notice, or afterwards on notice.

§ 407. If the impeachment is prosecuted on the petition of some citizen of the commonwealth, whose name is set at the foot of the articles of impeachment, he shall be liable for the costs of the accused if he is acquitted, and also for the costs of prosecuting the impeachment, and in that case the commonwealth shall not be liable to pay any part of the costs; and if the accused is convicted, the petitioner shall be entitled to recover of the accused the costs of the impeachment, for which he is liable; the costs to be taxed by the clerk of the senate, and recovered by suit in a court of competent jurisdiction.

# TITLE XII.

# PROCEEDINGS FOR THE REMOVAL FROM OFFICE OF CLERKS OF COURTS.

4. What clerks may be removed by the court of appeals. Causes of removal.

§ 409. There shall be a prosecutor generally.

§ 410. Court to fix a day of trial and summon the defendant.

§ 411. Clerk to issue subpoenas for witnesses, &c.

§ 412. Court may grant continuances.

§ 413. May require security for costs of prosecutor.

§ 414. How judgment for costs to be rendered.

§ 415. When clerk has been convicted of offense which forfeits his office, no prosecutor required.

# POR THE REMOVOL FROM OFFICE OF CLERKS OF COURTS.

335

# § 408. Court may appoint a clerk pro tem.

when the clerk of the court of appeals is tried.

# § 408. Clerks of the court of appeals, and clerks of circuit, county, and police and city courts, may be removed from office by the court of appeals, upon a written information signed and presented in said court by the attorney general, charging the following causes of removal:

1. Any act, omission, or neglect by such clerk, for which the statutes have prescribed a forfeiture of office.
2. Any malfeasance in office or neglect of official duty that the court may consider sufficient cause of removal.

# § 409.

Except in the case provided in section 415, there shall be a prosecutor responsible for costs named in the information. The information shall state the offense charged with the certainty required in an indictment; and if there be more than one offense they shall be stated separately and distinctly.

# § 410.

Upon the information being presented, the court shall fix a day of trial, and the clerk of the court of appeals shall issue a summons, warning the defendant to appear on that day and answer the information. The summons shall be served by delivering a copy of the summons, and of the information to the defendant, if he be found, and if not, to any deputy or white person remaining in his office by his employment, and if there be none, then by affixing such copies to the door of the clerk's office of the defendant.

# § 411.

The clerk of the court of appeals shall, on request of the attorney general or of the defendant, issue subpoenas for witnesses, and the court may order process for the production of books and papers, and may compel obedience to the subpoenas and other process, and compel witnesses to testify, by fine and imprisonment, to the same extent as circuit courts.

# FOR THE REMOVAL FROM OFFICE OF CLERKS OF COURTS.

§ 412. The court may grant continuances or postponement of the trial for sufficient cause.

§ 413. If the court is satisfied that the prosecutor is insolvent, or a non-resident, security for the costs may be required.

§ 414. If the court renders a judgment of removal from office against the clerk, a judgment shall also be rendered against him for the costs of the prosecutor; and if the clerk is acquitted, he shall recover a judgment against the prosecutor for his costs.

§ 415. Where a clerk has been convicted by the judgment of a court of competent jurisdiction of an offense, which by the provisions of a statute of Kentucky creates a forfeiture of his office, upon a transcript of the record of such conviction being produced to the attorney general, he shall forthwith file an information, without any prosecutor being named therein, against such clerk, and file therewith the transcript of such record, and thereupon the same proceedings had as upon informations with a prosecutor.

§ 416. Where an information is filed against the clerk of the court of appeals, the court may appoint a person to act as clerk during the prosecution, in all matters connected with the prosecution.

# SUMMONS AND WARNING ORDER.

# 1. In actions by ordinary proceedings.

The Commonwealth of Kentucky to the Sheriff of Franklin County.

You are commanded to summon Richard Jones to answer on the first day of the next October term of the Franklin circuit court, a petition filed against him in said court by John Smith, and warn him, that upon his failure to answer, the petition will be taken for confessed, or he will be proceeded against for contempt; and you will make due return of this summons on the first day of the next October term of said court.

Witness, P. Swigert, clerk of said court, this 10th day of September, 1850. P. SWIGERT, C. F. C. C.

# 2. In actions by equitable proceedings.

The Commonwealth of Kentucky to the Sheriff of Franklin County.

You are commanded to summon Richard Jones to answer in twenty days after the service of this summons on him, a petition filed against him in the Franklin circuit court by John Smith, and warn him, that upon his failure to answer, the petition will be taken for confessed, or he will be proceeded against for contempt; and you will make due return of this summons on the first day of the next October term of said court.

Witness, P. Swigert, clerk of said court, this 15th day of June, 1850. P. SWIGERT, C. F. C. C.

# 3. Summons by a justice of the peace, &c.

FRANKLIN COUNTY.

The Commonwealth of Kentucky to any Constable of Franklin County.

You are commanded to summon Thomas Jones to appear before me or some other justice of the peace of Franklin county, at our next September term.

against

1

John Smith, Plaintiff, Reply. FRANKLIN CIRCUIT COURT.

against

Richard Jones, Defendant.

The plaintiff, John Smith, admits that he made the contract in writing mentioned in the defendant's answer, but afterwards in the month of September, 1848, the plaintiff and defendant, by a verbal agreement, rescinded said contract, and defendant then agreed to restore to the plaintiff the possession of the land, on the first day of November, 1848, which the defendant has failed to do.

(Affidavit as before.) JOHN SMITH.

This answer is an example of a counter-claim. By the present law, the defendant could not set up this counter-claim in the action to recover the land, but would be compelled to file a bill in chancery for the specific performance of his contract of purchase, and obtain an injunction against the proceedings or judgment in the action for the land.

The reply is rendered necessary, by the counter-claim set up in the answer. This closes the pleadings, unless the defendant files a demurrer to the reply.

In this example the issue arises on the reply alone, and the defendant is considered as controverting the material statements of the reply, not only by denial but also by any matters in avoidance. Upon the trial, if the plaintiff proves the verbal contract as stated in the reply, the defendant would be permitted to prove that subsequently this verbal agreement was rescinded, or that it was obtained of him by fraud or duress.

The trial in this case is by the court, if either party moves for such a trial in pursuance of section 10.

# JUDGMENTS IN ORDINARY PROCEEDINGS.

By default in an action on a promissory note for the payment of money. The defendant having been duly summoned and failing to answer, it is adjudged by the court that the plaintiff recover of the defendant one hundred dollars, the amount claimed in the petition, with interest thereon at the rate of six per cent. per annum from the fifteenth of June, 1850, until paid, and his costs herein expended.

Where the answer is filed and a verdict rendered for the plaintiff. The parties appeared by their attorneys, and the defendant filed his answer to the petition of the plaintiff; and thereupon, the following jurors: A, B, C, D, E, F, G, H, I, J, K, and L, were sworn to try the issue, and having heard the evidence, rendered the following verdict: "We, of the jury, find for the plaintiff three hundred dollars. A. B., Foreman." It is therefore adjudged by the court that the plaintiff recover of the defendant three hundred dollars, with interest thereon at the rate of six per cent. per annum from the tenth of May, 1849, until paid, and his costs herein expended.

Demurrer to the petition adjudged good, and judgment rendered in bar of the action. The parties appeared by their attorneys, and the defendant filed a demurrer to the petition, and the questions of law arising thereon being heard, it is the opinion of the court that the petition is insufficient. And the plaintiff saying nothing further in the case, it is adjudged that he take nothing by his petition; and that the defendant recover of him his costs herein expended.

# For the conveyance and possession of a tract of land.

The parties appeared by their attorneys, and this action came on for trial on the pleadings, evidence, and exhibits. It is adjudged by the court that the defendant do, on or before the first day of January next, surrender to the plaintiff the possession of one hundred acres of land described in the petition, lying in Franklin county, on Benson creek. And that said defendant do also, on or before that time, convey to the plaintiff by deed containing clause of general warranty, said tract of land, and pay to the plaintiff his costs herein expended.

# Judgment in equitable actions where the defendant has answered.

This action coming on for trial, and the pleadings and proof having been duly considered by the court, it is adjudged, &c.

Note: The judgment in equitable actions are varied so much by the circumstances of each case, that it is considered useless to prepare forms, especially as those judgments are usually written out by the judge. Consequently, we have only prepared two forms of judgments in equitable actions, and the commencement of a judgment where an answer has been filed. It will be seen that even in a case of a judgment on the foreclosure of a mortgage, considerable variations as to the time and place of sale, and manner and time of advertisement, might be proper.

The Commonwealth of Kentucky, FRANKLIN CIRCUIT COURT.

against

John Smith.

The grand jury of Franklin county, in the name and by the authority of the commonwealth of Kentucky, accuse John Smith of the crime of murder, committed as follows, viz: The said John Smith, on the 10th day of July, 1854, in the county aforesaid, did feloniously and with malice aforethought, kill and murder Thomas Jones, by stabbing him with a knife, or some other edged weapon, [or, by shooting him with a gun or pistol, or by striking him with an axe or some other heavy weapon, according to the facts, against the peace and dignity of the commonwealth of Kentucky.

# No. 17. Indictment for embezzlement.

The Commonwealth of Kentucky,

against

John Smith.

The grand jury of Franklin county, in the name and by the authority of the commonwealth of Kentucky, accuse John Smith of the crime of embezzling, to his own use, bank notes and money, committed as follows, viz: The said John Smith, on the 10th day of July, 1854, in the county aforesaid, being a servant in the Farmers Bank of Kentucky, at Frankfort, a banking corporation created by the laws of Kentucky, did fraudulently convert to his own use twenty dollars in silver, and one hundred dollars in gold, the property of said bank; and which said money, thus appropriated, had been entrusted by John B. Temple, the cashier of said Farmers Bank of Kentucky, to said John Smith, who was a servant in the employment of said bank at that time, to be taken from said Farmers Bank, in Frankfort, to be deposited by him to the credit of E. H. Taylor, in the Bank of Kentucky in said town, against the peace and dignity of the commonwealth of Kentucky.

# 410 FORMS IN CRIMINAL CASES

# PLEADINGS OF THE DEFENDANT

# Demurrer

The Commonwealth of Kentucky, Plaintiff,

against

John Smith, Defendant.

The defendant demurs to the indictment.

# Plea

The Commonwealth of Kentucky, Plaintiff,

against

John Smith, Defendant.

The defendant pleads that he is guilty of the offense charged in the indictment.

Or,

The defendant pleads that he is not guilty of the offense charged in the indictment.

Or,

The defendant pleads that he has been acquitted of the offense charged in the indictment, by the judgment of the Franklin circuit court rendered on 1st day of March, 1854.

Or,

The defendant pleads that he has been convicted of the offense charged in the indictment, by the judgment of the Franklin circuit court rendered on the 1st day of March, 1854.

FORMS IN CRIMINAL CASES.

# ORDERS OF COURT.

# Order for process on indictment.

Commonwealth of Kentucky, Plaintiff,

against

John Smith, Defendant.

It is ordered that a bench warrant issue for the arrest of the defendant, allowing him to give bail in the sum of dollars.

Or, [if the offense is not bailable,]

It is ordered that a bench warrant issue for the arrest and committal to jail of the defendant. Or,

It is ordered that a summons issue on the indictment herein.

# Order for a bench warrant after the defendant has given bail.

Commonwealth of Kentucky, Plaintiff,

against

John Smith, Defendant.

It appearing that the defendant has failed to appear in pursuance of the undertaking of his bail, [or, that the bail taken herein is insufficient, or that the offense for which defendant is indicted is not bailable,] it is ordered that a bench warrant issue for the arrest of the defendant, allowing him to give bail in the sum of dollars, [or, that a bench warrant issue for the arrest and committal to jail of the defendant, if the offense is not bailable.

# Order upon ascertaining the defendant's true name.

The Commonwealth of Kentucky, Plaintiff,

against

John Smith, Defendant.

It appearing to the satisfaction of the court, that the defendant's name is John Jones, it is ordered that the further proceedings in this case shall be in defendant's true name of John Jones, indicted by the name of John Smith.

412  FORMS IN CRIMINAL CASES.

# Order setting aside an indictment and ordering the case to be submitted to another grand jury.

Commonwealth of Kentucky, Plaintiff,

against

John Smith, Defendant.

The defendant moved the court to set aside the indictment, on the ground that the grand jury had been illegally summoned and impaneled, [or, other ground, according to the facts,] and the court being sufficiently advised, it is ordered that the indictment be set aside, and the charge against the defendant submitted to another grand jury, and the defendant committed to jail to await the action of such grand jury, [or, that the defendant be admitted to give bail in the sum of dollars, for his appearance to answer the charge.

# Order for the removal of the defendant to another county, on its appearing that the offense was committed in such county.

Commonwealth of Kentucky, Plaintiff,

against

John Smith, Defendant.

It appearing to the satisfaction of the court, that the offense charged in the indictment was committed in Scott county, it is ordered that the jury herein be discharged, and that the clerk transmit to the clerk of the Scott circuit court a copy of the indictment and of this order, and also all the original papers of the prosecution, including the bail bond, (if any,) and that the sheriff carry and deliver the defendant, with a copy of this order, to the jailer of Scott county, [or, that the defendant be required to appear in the Scott circuit court to answer the charge, (if he has given bail,) or, that the defendant be allowed to give bail in the sum of —     - dollars, for his appearance in the Scott circuit court to answer the charge.

# Order upon its appearing that the offense was committed in another State.

Commonwealth of Kentucky, Plaintiff,

against

John Smith, Defendant.

It appearing to the satisfaction of the court, that the offense charged in the indictment was committed in the State of Tennessee, it is ordered that the jury herein be discharged, and that the defendant be committed to jail to await a requisition for him by the Governor of Tennessee.

# Judgment, where the punishment is death.

Commonwealth of Kentucky, Plaintiff,

against

John Smith, Defendant.

The defendant was this day brought into court, and being informed of the nature of the indictment, plea, and verdict, was asked if he had any legal cause to show why judgment should not be pronounced against him; and none being shown, it is adjudged by the court that the defendant be taken to the jail of Franklin county, and there safely kept until the 10th day of September, 1854, on which day, between sun rise and sun set, the sheriff of Franklin county shall hang him by the neck until he is dead.

# Judgment where the punishment is confinement in the penitentiary.

The Commonwealth of Kentucky, Plaintiff,

against

John Smith, Defendant.

The defendant was this day brought into court, and being informed of the nature of the indictment, plea, and verdict, was asked if he had any legal cause to show why judgment should not be pronounced against him; and none being shown, it is adjudged that the defendant be taken by the sheriff of Franklin county to the state penitentiary, and there confined at hard labor for the period of four years.

# Judgment for a fine and imprisonment.

The Commonwealth of Kentucky, Plaintiff,

against

John Smith, Defendant.

The jury impaneled herein returned the following verdict: "We of the jury, find the defendant guilty, and affix his punishment at a fine of one thousand dollars, and confinement in the county jail for six

Wherefore, it is adjudged that the plaintiff recover of the defendant the sum of one thousand dollars and the costs of this prosecution, and that the defendant be confined in the jail of Franklin county for the period of six months; and when said period of confinement shall have terminated, the defendant shall be retained in confinement until the fine aforesaid is paid, but the period of such confinement, for the non-payment of the fine, shall not exceed one day for each two dollars of the fine.

# Judgment of: removal from office, for malfeasance in office.

The Commonwealth of Kentucky, Plaintiff against John Smith, Sheriff of Franklin county, Defendant.

It appearing by the verdict that the defendant has been guilty of an offense which creates a forfeiture of his office of sheriff of Franklin county, it is adjudged that the defendant be removed from his office of sheriff of Franklin county, and said office is hereby adjudged to be vacant by said removal.

# APPEALS

# APPEALS TO THE COURT OF APPEALS

# Order noting the prayer for an appeal, in cases of felony, by the defendant.

The Commonwealth of Kentucky, Plaintiff,

against

John Smith, Defendant.

The defendant prayed an appeal from the judgment herein to the court of appeals.

# Order noting the prayer for an appeal, in cases of felony, by the commonwealth.

The Commonwealth of Kentucky, Plaintiff,

against

John Smith, Defendant.

The plaintiff prayed an appeal from the decision of court rendered herein, instructing the jury that they should find for the defendant, reciting the decision from which the appeal is prayed; or, if from the final judgment, the form is the same as is given for the defendant.

# Allowance of the appeal by a judge of the court of appeals.

Commonwealth of Kentucky, Plaintiff,

against

John Smith, Defendant.

The appeal prayed herein by the defendant is allowed.

Given under my hand as judge of the court of appeals of Kentucky, this 10th day of September, 1854.

T. A. MARSHALL

# Certificate or the appeal being taken in cases of felony.

# COURT OF APPEALS OF KENTUCKY

John Smith, Appellant,

against

The Commonwealth of Kentucky, Appellee.

I, Jacob Swigert, clerk of the court of appeals of Kentucky, do certify that John Smith has this day taken an appeal in the manner and form prescribed by law, from a judgment of the Franklin circuit court rendered at its October term, 1854, in the case of the commonwealth of Kentucky, plaintiff, against John Smith, defendant, for the confinement of the defendant in the state penitentiary at hard labor for the period of four years; it is therefore ordered that the execution of said judgment be suspended.

Witness my hand this 10th October, 1854.

J. SWIGERT, Clerk.

# Order granting an appeal in cases of misdemeanor.

The Commonwealth of Kentucky, Plaintiff,

against

John Smith, Defendant.

The defendant prayed an appeal from the judgment herein to the court of appeals, which is granted upon condition that he lodge in the clerk's office of said court a transcript of the record in sixty days from the date of the judgment. The same form may be used where the appeal is prayed by the plaintiff.

# Supersedeas bond in cases of misdemeanor.

# FRANKLIN CIRCUIT COURT

The Commonwealth of Kentucky, Plaintiff,

against

John Smith, Defendant.

The defendant, John Smith, having prayed an appeal from the judgment of the Franklin circuit court, rendered herein against him at its October term, 1854, for a fine of one thousand dollars and costs, and imprisonment in the jail of Franklin county for six months, now we covenant to and with the plaintiff that in case said judgment is affirmed, we will pay said fine and costs, and all damages thereon, and the costs.

# ARBITRATION AND AWARDS—

Proceedings to be according to chapter 3 of Revised Statutes--

1. Manner of submitting controversy by rule of court.
2. The arbitrators to be sworn. Their oath. May examine parties on oath.
3. May issue subpoenas for witnesses, and report to court the failure of witnesses to attend or testify.
4. Personal representatives, committees, and guardians may make a submission.
5. If arbitrator refuses to act, reference may be set aside.
6. Trial before arbitrators, and their award.
7. How the award to be made the judgment of the court.
8. No award to be set aside for defect of form. Courts of equity to have jurisdiction over awards.
9. Matter in justices' jurisdiction may be submitted by rule or orders in justices' court, on which same proceedings may be had. Subject to appeal

When depositions may be taken. Amendments of caveat and response. When the caveat is to be deemed abandoned. Plaintiff may be required to give security for costs. 2. # SETTLEMENT OF ACCOUNTS OF ADMINISTRATORS, GUARDIANS, &C., IN COUNTY COURT-

The accounts of personal representatives, guardians, committees of idiots and lunatics, and trustees, to be settled as prescribed in article 22 of chapter 27 Revised Statutes.

1. Presiding judge to make settlements. His compensation. If judge cannot act, the clerk may make the settlement. Judge may appoint a standing committee to settle.
2. In making settlements, he may hear evidence. Must reduce verbal evidence to writing. To allow no credit without evidence. His report must show the debits and credits, and the vouchers and evidence to sustain them. Report to lie over one term for exceptions. If no exceptions, report to be recorded, if approved. On exceptions report may be altered. Vouchers to be filed. New evidence in court must be reduced to writing. Settlements prima facie evidence. Notice must be given to those interested, who reside in the county. Witnesses may be summoned and attendance coerced. May adjourn from time to time. May interrogate fiduciary on oath. No evidence presumed except that reported.

Trustees may settle in the county court where the deed or will is recorded.

In such case the examination shall be before two justices; and—

1. If they cannot concur that there are reasonable grounds of guilt, defendant shall be discharged.
2. If they concur, held for trial.
3. If they differ as to amount of bail, it shall be taken in smaller sum.
4. If they differ on other questions, the opinion more favorable to defendant shall prevail.
5. Justice before whom defendant first brought, shall return papers, etc.

5. An attorney, concerning any communication made to him by his client in that relation, or his advice thereon, without the client's consent.
6. Persons interested in an issue, in behalf of themselves, and parties to an issue, in behalf of themselves or those united with them in the issue.
7. Negroes, mulattoes, or Indians, in any action or proceeding where a white person, in his own right, or

# EVIDENCE

as representative of a white person, is a party, except in actions brought to recover a penalty or forfeiture for a violation of law, against a negro, mulatto, or indian.

§ 671. All other objections to witnesses shall go to their credit alone, and be weighed by the jury or tribunal to which their evidence is offered.

Defendant sets up a counter-claim, and judgment rendered in his favor. The parties appeared by their attorneys, and the defendant filed an answer setting up a counter-claim against the plaintiff, to which plaintiff filed a reply; and thereupon the following jurors: A, B, C, D, E, F, G, H, I, J, K, and L, were sworn to try the issue, (or issues,) and having heard the evidence, rendered the following verdict: "We, of the jury, find for the defendant three hundred and seventy-five dollars in damages. A. B., Foreman." It is therefore adjudged by the court that the defendant recover of the plaintiff three hundred and seventy-five dollars, and his costs herein expended.

Action for personal property. Judgment for plaintiff. The parties appeared by their attorneys, and the defendant filed his answer to the petition; and thereupon the following jurors: A, B, C, D, E, F, G, H, I, J, K, and L, were sworn to try the issue, (or issues,) and having heard the evidence, rendered the following verdict: "We, of the jury, find for the plaintiff the negro man Joe, of the value of

*If it is a case in which interest is allowed, that should be inserted. In many cases the law and facts will be submitted to the court without the intervention of a jury; in such cases the above form may be varied accordingly.

references.source_filename: Code of Practice in Civil and Criminal Cases, for the State of Kentucky -1854.pdf
references.document_author: M. C. Johnson, James Harlan, and J. W. Stevenson,
references.context:
# FORMS UNDER THE CIVIL CODE.

eight hundred dollars, and one hundred dollars in damages for his detention. A. B., Foreman. It is therefore adjudged by the court that the plaintiff recover of the defendant the negro man Joe, in the petition mentioned, if to be had; if not, eight hundred dollars, his value; and one hundred dollars damages for his detention; also, his costs herein expended.

Joint action against administrator and heirs on the contract of their ancestor. Answer by administrator that he had fully administered the assets; and by the heirs that they had received nothing by descent. The parties appeared by their attorneys, and the defendant A. B., as administrator, and E, F, G, and H, as heirs of C. D., deceased, filed their several answers to the petition of the plaintiff, and thereupon the following jurors: A, B, C, D, E, F, G, H, I, J, K, and L, were sworn to try the issues, and having heard the evidence, rendered the following verdict: "We, of the jury, find for the defendant, A. B., administrator, on the issue tendered in his answer; and for the plaintiff on the issue between himself and the heirs of C. D., deceased; and that they have received by descent from their ancestor, C. D., real estate of the value of five hundred dollars. A. B., Foreman." It is therefore adjudged by the court that the plaintiff recover of the defendant, A. B., administrator, and E, F, G, and H, heirs of C. D., deceased, five hundred dollars, with interest at the rate of six per cent. per annum from the first of July, 1849, until paid, and his costs herein expended, to be levied of the assets of his intestate which may hereafter come to his hands to be administered, if sufficient; if not, then to be levied of the real estate descended to the defendants, the heirs, from C. D., deceased.
